title: State to tap money markets for more than $100bn
full_text:
BUDGET 2026: INTEREST COSTS Victoria will tap money markets for more than $100bn over the next four years, with new figures showing the next wave of borrowing could lock in more than $7bn a year in interest costs as it grapples with a refinancing burden from cheap pandemic-era bonds.
Analysis of Treasury Corporation of Victoria figures reveals a major shift in Victoria’s debt profile, with the refinancing of existing debt rising from about a third of its borrowing last year to almost a half next year.
The pressure on the state’s finances comes as net debt is forecast to hit $199.3bn by the end of the decade, and the Allan government is claiming the state opposition’s plan to rein in borrowing would require cuts to frontline services.
Victoria expects to raise $125.5bn in debt over the next four years, including $59.5bn in new borrowing and $66bn to refinance maturing bonds.
Borrowing at current market rates – with TCV’s 10-year bonds yielding 5.7 per cent – would result in an annual interest bill of $7.2bn.
The refinancing component alone would add more than $2bn a year in interest costs, compared with debt issued during the pandemic at rates closer to 1.5 to 2 per cent.
Jess Wilson said the Allan government had “abandoned any pretence of debt reduction and responsible fiscal management”.
“As cheap Covid-era borrowings fall due, Labor has confirmed it will continue its borrowing binge – driving Victoria’s $1 million-an-hour interest repayments even higher,” the Opposition Leader said. “Turning around Victoria’s finances won’t be easy, but my team is up to the task and has a comprehensive plan to grow our economy and return Victoria to a cash surplus by 2032.” A government spokesperson said Ms Wilson’s “$40bn in cuts can’t happen without cutting hospitals and schools – because that’s where most of the state budget goes”.
“Labor is reducing debt as a share of GSP responsibly by growing the economy and delivering surpluses,” they said.
The projections show interest costs are rising sharply, forecast to almost double from $6.8bn in 2024-25 to $11.8bn by 2029-30, as cheap borrowing rolls off and is replaced at significantly higher rates.
Refinancing does not occur at a single uniform rate, but the scale of the new borrowing required highlights why the government is projecting interest payments to soar.
In recent years, refinancing only comprised about a third of the government’s borrowing – but this will rise to more than a half from 2027-28.
About $60bn of Victoria’s fixed-rate debt will mature between 2026 and 2030. A $10.7bn bond issued at just 1.25 per cent will fall due in November 2027, while a further $14.4bn line priced at 1.5 per cent matures in 2030.
Another low-interest bond – worth roughly $19.7bn with an interest rate of just 1.5 per cent – is up in September 2031. Ms Wilson announced a 10-year fiscal repair strategy, anchored by cutting $22bn in spending by imposing a hiring freeze on bureaucrats.
summary: Victoria plans to borrow over $100 billion in the next four years, leading to annual interest costs exceeding $7 billion as it faces a significant refinancing burden from maturing low-interest pandemic-era bonds.
author: Anthony Galloway, Victorian politic
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Victoria's net debt is projected to reach $199.3 billion by the end of the decade.
Interest costs are expected to rise from $6.8 billion in 2024-25 to $11.8 billion by 2029-30.
The opposition claims the government's borrowing strategy will necessitate cuts to essential services.
